coredata.dc:title: Functionalized sulfonic acid groups enhance solubility and stability of graphene quantum dots for efficient photosynthesis of lettuce
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Wu, Zhongqiu

Xu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots exhibit outstanding fluorescence properties, low cytotoxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility when interacting with plant cells, showcasing significant potential for photosynthesis of plants, such as lettuce. However, the poor dispersion and stability in aqueous solutions have hindered its photosynthetic efficiency. To address this issue, this work demonstrated the sulfonic acid group functionalized graphene quantum dots (SAG-GQDs), in which the sulfonic acid group prominently increased hydrophilicity, thus leading to an enhanced dispersion and stability of SAG-GQDs for efficient lettuce photosynthesis. Results indicated that the functionalized SAG-GQDs with spraying concentration of 3600 mg/L displayed the best photosynthetic efficiency for lettuce growth, including net photosynthetic rate of 8.6 μmol CO2 m−2 s−1, increased leaf fresh weight of 36%, and nutritional 14 mg/g soluble sugar with biocompatibility availability. Our work suggests the feasibility of functionalized sulfonic acid group in regulating dispersion and stability of graphene quantum dots for promoting photosynthetic efficiency of lettuce plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfonic acid group
Graphene quantum dots
Photosynthesis
Efficiency
Lettuce

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of SAG-GQDs on lettuce growth

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of enhancing photosynthesis of lettuce leaves by spraying SAG-GQDs.

(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectrum, and (c) FTIR spectra of the as-synthesized SAG-GQDs. (d) The PL spectra of SAG-GQDs at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (e) TEM image, and (f) column diagram of particle size of SAG-GQDs.
High-resolution XPS analysis of (a) survey, (b) C1s, (c) S2p, and (d) O1s spectra.
Mapping profiles of (a) C, (b) O, (c) S elements, and (d) EDS spectra of SAG-GQDs.
Effects of spraying concentrations of SAG-GQDs on the appearance of lettuce leaves.

Effects of spraying concentrations of SAG-GQDs on photosynthetic parameters of lettuce. (a) Photosynthetic rate, (b) stomatal conductance, (c) intercellular CO2 concentration, and (d) transpiration rate.
Effects of spraying concentrations of SAG-GQDs on growth indicators of lettuce leaves. (a) Leaf and root fresh weight, and (b) leaf and root length.

Effects of spraying concentrations of SAG-GQDs on nutritional index of lettuce. (a) Leaf and root dry weight, (b) soluble sugar content, (c) soluble protein content, and (d) nitrate nitrogen contents.

CLSM image of the lettuce leaves treated by different methods. (a) Control group, without spraying of SAG-GQDs, (b) lettuce leave surface sprayed with 3600 mg/L SAG-GQDs, (c) after spraying for ten days, and (d) directly washing leaves with water.

head.ce:title: Functionalized sulfonic acid groups enhance solubility and stability of graphene quantum dots for efficient photosynthesis of lettuce

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Vocational College of Electronics and Information, Huainan, Jiangsu 223003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Vocational College of Electronics and Information

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Vocational College of Electronics and Information, Huainan, Jiangsu 223003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots exhibit outstanding fluorescence properties, low cytotoxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility when interacting with plant cells, showcasing significant potential for photosynthesis of plants, such as lettuce. However, the poor dispersion and stability in aqueous solutions have hindered its photosynthetic efficiency. To address this issue, this work demonstrated the sulfonic acid group functionalized graphene quantum dots (SAG-GQDs), in which the sulfonic acid group prominently increased hydrophilicity, thus leading to an enhanced dispersion and stability of SAG-GQDs for efficient lettuce photosynthesis. Results indicated that the functionalized SAG-GQDs with spraying concentration of 3600 mg/L displayed the best photosynthetic efficiency for lettuce growth, including net photosynthetic rate of 8.6 μmol CO2 m−2 s−1, increased leaf fresh weight of 36%, and nutritional 14 mg/g soluble sugar with biocompatibility availability. Our work suggests the feasibility of functionalized sulfonic acid group in regulating dispersion and stability of graphene quantum dots for promoting photosynthetic efficiency of lettuce plants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Agricultural sustainability and food security are major concerns around the world [1]. To meet the demand for food and agricultural products from a growing global population, increasing the photosynthesis yield and quality of crops has become a critical objective [2]. However, traditional agricultural practices have low photosynthetic efficiencies due to factors such as poor soil quality [3], inefficient water resource use [4], and environmental pollution [5], which are no longer sufficient to meet the demand for increased food production. Therefore, more efficient methods to enhance crop yield are urgently needed.

Graphene quantum dots assisted photosynthesis has been a prominent topic for harnessing sunlight and accumulating organic compounds for crop growth [6]. For example, Chandra et al. extracted chloroplasts from plant leaves and introduced them into carbon quantum dots solution to enhance the photosynthesis process [7], where the carbon quantum dots can act as electron donors in combination with chloroplasts. More importantly, the electrons received by chloroplasts can be transported to photosystems, thus benefiting plant growth. Moreover, Hu et al. investigated the impact of carbon quantum dots on photosynthetic pigments and elucidated their role by measuring chlorophyll content, spectral absorption, and fluorescence spectra [8], which further illustrated that carbon quantum dots can be designed to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of pigments and improve sun energy conversion in chloroplast membranes. Nonetheless, the nonpolar nature of CC and CH bonds in these carbon quantum dots due to the lack of distinct charge differences, results in high hydrophobicity [9]. This creates challenges related to poor dispersion and stability of carbon quantum dots in aqueous solutions, leading to low photosynthetic efficiency. Thus, addressing the poor water solubility of these materials and leverage the surface functionalization of carbon quantum dots are of great significance [10].

In this work, we have demonstrated sulfonic acid group functionalized graphene quantum dots (designed as SAG-GQDs) as a well-dispersed and hydrophilic material for enhancing lettuce photosynthesis. The principle of enhancing lettuce photosynthetic systems by uniformly spraying SAG-GQDs onto the leaf surface was illustrated in Fig. 1 , where the modification of sulfonic acid group provided highly dispersed negative charge, and enhanced the stability of quantum dots in aqueous solutions, thus improving the dispersion with stability [11]. Moreover, after spraying SAG-GQDs on lettuce leaves, the photosynthetic performance exhibited significant enhancement, including photosynthetic parameters of net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, intercellular CO2 concentration, transpiration rate, growth indicators of fresh and dried root weight, and nutritional index of soluble sugar, protein, and nitrate nitrogen contents. Our work demonstrated that the feasibility of sulfonic acid-modified graphene quantum dots for regulating its dispersion and stability for increasing photosynthetic efficiency of lettuce growth.

The SAG-GQDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method [12]. The primary procedure involved mixing citric acid, taurine, and double-distilled water in a mass ratio of 1:0.7:5 in a container. After stirring and dissolving, the solution was dried at 60 °C without moisture remained. Subsequently, the container was transferred to a high-temperature oven, and maintained at 180–200 °C for 10–12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the intermediate products were collected, which was then redistributed in deionized water. The sodium hydroxide solution was then added until the solution reached pH of 7. The mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and subjected to dialysis using a 3 kDa molecular weight cutoff membrane in ultrapure water for 24 h. Ultimately, the obtained solution was frozen at −20 °C for 24 h to obtain the desired graphene quantum dots [13].

The morphology and dimensions of SAG-GQDs were observed using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). The adsorbed molecular structures of SAG-GQDs were characterized using a fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet iS50) spectrometer. The crystal structure of SAG-GQDs was analyzed using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Smart Lab). The crystal composition was analyzed using Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fischer DXR). The chemical composition of SAG-GQDs was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5300) with Mg Kα radiation. The amount of sulfur was determined using SEM (Zeiss Gemini 300) that coupled with energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The presence and distribution of SAG-GQDs dots on the surface of lettuce leaves were observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica TCS SP5).

The experiment was conducted at the planting base of the College of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University. The cultivation environment at the base was precisely controlled at 25 °C, and the relative humidity was around 50% [14]. The full-spectrum LED light sources were used for illumination. The lettuces were placed 10 cm away from the LED light during the seedling stage. The photoperiod was set to 16 h light/8 h dark, with a light intensity of 4 W. Lettuce seeds were initially sown in moist soil. After reaching the four-leaf stage, the healthy lettuce seedlings were selected and transplanted into 20-liter cultivation containers. The nutrient solution for cultivating lettuce was Hoagland's nutrient solution, with an initial electrical conductivity value of 1.50 mS/cm and a pH of 6.8. A hydroponic system was utilized, allowing precise control of environmental factors such as pH, temperature, and nutrient concentration [15]. After transplantation, SAG-GQDs aqueous solutions were prepared at different concentrations (0, 1200, 2400, 3600, and 4800 mg/L). The lettuce foliage was sprayed every five days with the SAG-GQDs solution according to the lettuce growth habits, which allowed for effective uptake and gradual release of the SAG-GQDs for reducing risk of toxicity. After cultivating for 21 days, the lettuce foliage was harvested and used for measurement of photosynthetic parameters, such as growth indicator and nutritional index.

The photosynthesis-fluorescence measurement system (LI-6400XT) was utilized to assess the photosynthetic parameters of lettuce. The measured indices included net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, intercellular CO2 concentration, and transpiration rate [16]. To measure the photosynthetic parameters of lettuce leaves, samples were prepared and placed in the sample chamber, ensuring complete coverage to prevent gas leakage. Parameters, such as light intensity, CO2 concentration, and temperature were set before initiating the measurement process. Upon completion, data were collected and analyzed to assess the physiological status and photosynthetic efficiency of the lettuce leaves [17].

From each experimental group, lettuce plants were selected using a random sampling method. Before test, wiping the residual water and impurities from the surface of the plant, and draining the surface water of the plant roots. The lengths of each lettuce leaf and root were individually measured using a ruler. Subsequently, root bases of the plants were severed, and used for recording both fresh and dry weights. Fresh weight was measured using an electronic balance. For dry weight, the samples were first dehydrated in a 105 °C oven for 1 h, then dried at 75 °C until a constant weight was achieved [18]. The final dry weight was also determined using an electronic balance.

The nutritional index included soluble sugar, protein, and nitrate nitrogen contents. Soluble sugar content was determined using the anthrone colorimetric method [19]. Fresh samples were extracted in distilled water, and the filtrate was collected and diluted. A portion of the extract was mixed with anthrone-acetic acid reagent and concentrated sulfuric acid. Soluble protein content was determined using the Coomassie Brilliant Blue method [20]. Fresh samples were homogenized in distilled water, centrifuged, and the resulting supernatant was mixed with Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 solution. For nitrate nitrogen content, plants were homogenized and extracted in distilled water by heated for 30 min. After cooling and filtration, the extract was mixed with salicylic-sulfuric acid and NaOH solutions [21]. Finally, the absorbance of the resulting solutions was measured using a spectrophotometer.

In this experiment, each treatment within every experimental group was replicated for three times. The results were presented using mean values ± standard deviation (SD) and depicted graphically through bar charts.

The crystal structure of the synthesized SAG-GQDs was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD). A sharp peak at 2θ = 23° (Fig. 2 a) corresponded to the (002) crystal face of carbon, indicating that the material consisted of highly crystalline [22]. The sharpness of the diffraction peak may be attributed to the high temperature synthesis with presence of sulfonic acid functionalization [23,24]. Moreover, the Raman spectrum reflected the degree of graphitization of the as-synthesized SAG-GQDs (Fig. 2b). As observed, a D peak emerged at 1340 cm−1, and a G peak appears at 1600 cm−1, with an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.71. This ratio suggested a low defect level in the as-synthesized SAG-GQDs [25], indicating a highly ordered graphitic structure.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of SAG-GQDs was then demonstrated (Fig. 2c), where the absorption peaks observed at 3540, 3270, and 1490 cm−1 corresponded well to the stretching vibrations of COH, OH, and CC, respectively [26,27]. In addition, the peaks at 1190 and 1040 cm−1 represented the vibrations of C-O and the sulfur-containing functional group (SO3), respectively [28,29]. The presence of −SO3 and C-O indicated the successful modification of sulfonic acid groups upon SAG-GQDs. This modification enhanced surface electronegativity of SAG-GQDs due to the negative charge of sulfonic acid groups, rendering quantum dots more stable in aqueous solutions for dispersion and stability [30]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of SAG-GQDs exhibited exhibit blue fluorescence under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2d). Studies have shown that chloroplasts strongly absorb light between 400 and 500 nm as well as between 650 and 700 nm [31], indicating a good overlap between the emission spectrum of SAG-GQDs in the blue light region and the absorption spectrum of chloroplasts. The capability of absorbing ultraviolet light and emitting blue light suggested a potential way of SAG-GQDs for converting ultraviolet light into visible light region, thereby enhancing the efficiency of light conversion utilized by chloroplasts. The morphology of SAG-GQDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The prepared SAG-GQDs exhibited a well-defined spherical particle morphology without obvious aggregation (Fig. 2e). The average size of SAG-GQDs was estimated to be ∼ 10 nm (Fig. 2f).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of SAG-GQDs were demonstrated, where the binding energies at 172, 289, and 536 eV can be attributed to S2p, C1s and O1s (Fig. 3 a). In detail, the spectrum of C1s presented two peaks at 284.8 and 289 eV (Fig. 3b), attributing to C-C and C=O species [32]. The spectrum of S2p (Fig. 3c) can be decomposed into 166.5 and 168.7 eV, ascribing to the CSC and SO3H species. Thus, it was evident that that sulfonic groups have been successfully introduced into the SAG-GQDs [33]. Furthermore, the two peaks located at 531.8 and 535.8 eV can be observed in O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), which belonged to the CO and OCO species, respectively [34]. In addition, the element mapping profiles confirmed the co-existence of C, O, and S over the as-synthesized SAG-GQDs (Fig. 4 a-c). Moreover, the EDS spectra indicate the weight percentage of 11.64% and atomic percentage of 5.42% of sulfur (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SAG-GQDs on lettuce growth
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of spraying concentrations of SAG-GQDs on plant appearance were investigated (Fig. 5 ). After spraying with different concentrations of SAG-GQDs, it was visually evident that SAG-GQDs at each concentration exerted a certain degree of growth promotion on lettuce. Notably, lettuce that treated with 3600 mg/L SAG-GQDs spray exhibited the most prosperous growth according to the lettuce growth morphology.

The impacts of SAG-GQDs treatments at different concentrations on the photosynthetic parameters of lettuce were then investigated. In comparison to the control group (0 mg/L), lettuce treated with SAG-GQDs in the concentration range of 1200–3600 mg/L exhibited a noticeable increase in net photosynthetic rate (Fig. 6 a), with a slightly decreased group treated with 4800 mg/L. Within the concentration range of 0–3600 mg/L, the photosynthetic rate of lettuce was positively correlated with stomatal conductance (Fig. 6b). However, a deviation from this trend occurred in lettuce treated with higher concentrations of SAG-GQDs, possibly due to the impact of the high mass concentration of SAG-GQDs on the leaves that decreased the stomatal opening [35]. In addition, lettuces absorbed CO2 and consumed water during photosynthesis [36], thus the relationship between transpiration and photosynthesis was then investigated (Fig. 6c-d). With observation, the intercellular CO2 concentration (Fig. 6c) and transpiration rate (Fig. 6d) of lettuce exhibited a similar trend toward the net photosynthetic rate in different experimental groups, where the concentration of 3600 mg/L led to the optimal intercellular CO2 concentration (320 μL/L, or 8.6 μmol m−2 s−1) and transpiration rate (1.2 mmol H2O m−2 s−1).

The effects of different concentrations of SAG-GQDs on fresh weight of lettuce was followed to be illustrated. It was apparent that both average fresh weight (Fig. 7 a) and length (Fig. 7b) exhibit a positive correlation with the concentration of SAG-GQDs. The maximum values reached at a concentration of 3600 mg/L, with an increase of 36% in leaf fresh weight in comparison to control group. This phenomenon indicated that SAG-GQDs at a concentration of 3600 mg/L were most effective in enhancing lettuce growth.

The nutritional index of lettuce treated with different concentrations of SAG-GQDs were further investigated. In comparison to control group, the aboveground dry weight (Fig. 8 a) of lettuce increased in all experimental groups, with the 3600 mg/L treatment showing the most significant increase. However, the 1200 mg/L and 2400 mg/L experimental groups exhibited minor differences in elevating dry weight. Since the organic substances formed through photosynthesis constituted over 90% of the plant's dry weight [37], making the measurement of organic parameter representative for nutritional status. Sugar, the primary product of photosynthesis, showing changeable soluble sugar content (14 mg/g) after spraying SAG-GQDs in lettuce (Fig. 8b). Moreover, all experimental groups showed a significant increase in soluble protein content (Fig. 8c), with the 2400 mg/L and 3600 mg/L groups experiencing increments of 41.6% and 33.3%, respectively. Furthermore, the nitrate content after spraying SAG-GQDs was significantly affected (Fig. 8d), in which lettuce treated with 2400 mg/L showed the lowest nitrate content, reduced by 27% compared to the control group. Additionally, lettuce treated with 3600 mg/L SAG-GQDs exhibited an inverse relationship between nitrate content and the content of soluble proteins and sugars. The above results suggested that the spraying of SAG-GQDs on lettuce leaves both increased the photosynthetic efficiency, growth weights and nutritional values.

The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to observe and analyze the absorption and desorption of SAG-GQDs on lettuce surfaces under 405 nm light excitation. Compared to the control group (Fig. 9 a), the fluorescence of spraying SAG-GQDs uniformly labeled the structure and shape of lettuce leaves (Fig. 9b), indicating SAG-GQDs that sprayed onto the lettuce surface were absorbed by the leaves and evenly distributed within the veins and mesophyll [38]. In contrast, after spraying for 10 days, the fluorescence signal was notably decreased (Fig. 9c), with only faint signals observed in the stomata. This phenomenon indicated the potential elimination of exogenous SAG-GQDs by the leaves over time. Possible reasons could be attributed to the metabolism of SAG-GQDs by lettuce leaves into other compounds or molecules [39]. More importantly, after further washing with purified water, the signal of SAG-GQDs was absent on the leaf surface (Fig. 9d), indicating the water-removability of SAG-GQDs from the lettuce leaves. The above results confirmed the safety and biocompatibility of SAG-GQDs for leaves growth, highlighting an efficient and environmentally friendly approach for efficient photosynthesis of lettuce.

In summary, this work has developed a well-dispersed SAG-GQDs in augmenting the growth and nutritional quality of lettuce through the enhancement of photosynthetic processes. The unique solubility and stability facilitated the absorption of SAG-GQDs by lettuce leaves, which significantly enhanced the photosynthetic parameters of lettuce, including photosynthetic parameters, growth indicators, and nutritional index of soluble contents with safety and biocompatibility. This work emphasized the promising potential of SAG-GQDs as an innovative agricultural tool for enhancing crop growth, yield, and nutritional quality.

Junjie Yuan: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zongli Yang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiayi Zou: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhongqiu Wu: Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhaolong Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Liang Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenlong Shen: Data curation, Conceptualization. Quan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Hui Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFB4204600), Jiangsu Provincial Agricultural Science and Technology Independent Innovation Fund (CX(21)3067), Huai'an City Key research and development projects (HAN202212), Jiangsu Province Carbon Peak Carbon Neutrality Science and Technology Innovation Special Major Key Technology Research Project in Agriculture and Rural Areas (BE2022310), Launch funding for high-level talent researcher of Jiangsu University (5501510024).
